data.title: RenaissanceRe: Resilient Despite Wildfires But Hurricane Season Looms
data.description:
Shares of RenaissanceRe (NYSE:RNR) have been a solid performer over the past year, adding about 10%. Still, the stock is down over 10% from its highs before the California wildfires increased loss expectations. Q1 results showed the damage created by these fires, though the outlook was more encouraging. I last covered shares in January, rating the stock a “hold.” Since then, shares are up 1%, in keeping with my view that they were around fair value. However, that has been good enough to materially outperform the market’s 7% decline. I still view them as a hold.

In the company’s first quarter reported on April 23rd, RenRe lost $1.49, which was $0.64 worse than expectations. RNR had $3.4 billion of net written premiums, up from $3.2 billion a year ago. The first quarter was, of course, significantly impacted by the California wildfires, more than offsetting ongoing strength in investment income and share repurchases. The fires proved to be a bit more costly than I had anticipated, and they were the primary driver of the miss. Ultimately, catastrophe losses can be very lumpy, but I prefer a miss driven by one extreme outcome rather than recurring weakness in underwriting results.

Large loss events had a $1.35 billion impact before taxes, of this California wildfires were $1.255 billion. The net impact after taxes and reinstatement premiums was $633 million from California and $703 million all-in. This means that the California wildfires were a $13 per share impact—I had expected the loss to be closer to $11-12 per share. I think it is important to note that RenRe would have earned $11.50 absent the wildfire. Reinsurers have a reputation for very volatile earnings, given their exposure to these large catastrophe events.

This reputation definitely is earned to a degree, as these events have dramatic and periodic impacts on earnings. However, I think it bears emphasizing that in Q1 we experienced one of the worst catastrophes in the past twenty years. Still, the company ran close to break even. Thanks to higher pricing and increased diversification in the business, major cats significantly impair earnings, but they do not threaten the solvency of the company. Plus, we may see RNR be able to recoup some of these losses, via subrogation of the Eaton fire. Given the exact cause of that fire is not known, RNR has not assumed any ability to seek legal recovery yet, but that could be a source of upside.

Overall, RNR had a 128.3% combined ratio, resulting in a $771 million underwriting loss. There was a 52.6% impact from large loss events. Its combined ratio was up from 77.9% last year with essentially the entire increase explained by large losses. Typically, Q1 is among the more profitable quarters because cat losses are most severe during the US hurricane season, but the CA wildfires disrupted this normal seasonality.

Looking into results by units, in its property segment, net premiums rose by 21% to $1.7 billion. There was a $362 million increase in reinstatement premiums as the losses from the wildfire fully used up insurers’ policies, requiring them to renew a lapsed policy. This is a normal occurrence after a significant loss event. This unit is home to the vast majority of the company’s cat risk, and its adjusted combined ratio rose by 107% from 40.5% to 147.1%.

Property catastrophe’s combined ratio was 176%, and 90% of all losses came from California. About 80% of property premiums were catastrophe related in the quarter. This is a bit higher than normal, given the benefit from reinstatement premiums. I would note the developments in prior year was 15% favorable, better than last year’s 10% prior favorable development. These reserve releases are consistent the company’s history of conservatism. Overall, there was a 113.5% impact from the California wildfires, and the unit lost $607 million from a profit of $534 million last year.

In casualty, net premiums fell by 2.7% to $1.75 billion. The unit swung from a $6 million underwriting profit to a $163 million loss. Casualty and specialty had a combined ratio of 111.1%, including a 9.2% impact from large loss events. This was up 11.7% from last year. This segment’s results were a bit weaker than I would like to see as it implies a combined ratio above 100%, even excluding the wildfires.

Going forward, the combined ratio in Casualty is likely to be in the upper 90% area rather than the mid-90% target, given ongoing social inflation. This is a reason why RNR is allowing net premiums to fall, as policy economics are simply not as attractive, given increased costs. Conversely, after a large catastrophe event, we typically see improvement in property policies. On the earnings call, management noted that is exactly what is happening. The renewal environment has tilted towards insurers since January. Florida pricing is particularly strong, and significant legislative changes are starting to bend the cost curve. Catastrophe is stronger than other property.

Finally, the company’s fee income fell from $84 million to $30 million, given -$15 million of performance fees as the California wildfires impacts the performance of structured reinsurance products. Last year’s performance was abnormally strong, and I view $45-$50 million as a more reasonable run-rate going forward.

Elsewhere, RNR remains a beneficiary of elevated interest rates. The company had $405 million of net investment income, up from $391 million last year, realizing a 5.1% net investment income return. It has a $33.6 billion investment portfolio, which is predominately in fixed income. The fixed maturity portfolio has a 5.2% yield and a 2.7 year duration. This yield on fixed income was down by 20bps sequentially, given a lower fed funds rate. I expect we will see investment yields peak mid-year and then very gradual decline.

While the company has largely invested its portfolio in fixed income, insurers are structurally short inflation. Inflation can increase the cost of rebuilding, leading to more severe claims. Over time, RNR can mitigate this by raising premiums, but given most premiums are annual, there is always some short-term risk. As such, given inflation risk from tariffs, RNR allocated a portion of its portfolio to gold futures late last year. With gold continuing to outperform so dramatically since “Liberation Day,” this will be a nice tailwind to Q2 results.

RNR maintains a strong balance sheet, with $2.9 billion of debt for a modest 22% debt to capital ratio. Given its strong capital position, RNR executed on $361 million of Q1 buybacks and another $65 million of buybacks in April. Thanks to these repurchases, there has been a 6.4% share count reduction over the past year.

Back in January, I was targeting about $30 in 2025 earnings power. This expectation assumed about $11 of California wildfire risk. CA turned out to be $2 worse than I expected. I was also assuming about 10% premium growth, but given relative softness in casualty pricing, I expect growth to be below 5%, excluding reinstatement premiums. As such, I am now looking for about $25 of earnings this year, which assumes a normal hurricane season.

I have valued the reinsurers at 8-10x, given the volatility of their results. This led me to a hold previously while targeting $230 as a good buy level. We did see shares dip below that during the early March market panic and subsequently rebound. While wildfires were initially a bigger loss than expected, the fact RNR nearly broke even still speaks to the improved business mix it has achieved.

Plus, this is likely to reflect a worse than normal catastrophe year, barring a very quiet hurricane season, with underlying earnings power likely north of $30. As such, I do think RNR should trade towards the high-end of the valuation rate, making ~$250 a fair level. I am always cautious about buying reinsurers before the hurricane season as that can be a source of volatility and the potential for storm losses will likely cap shares near-term. As such, at $250, I view shares as a hold. If we saw the stock move back to $270, I would be a seller ahead of the hurricane season, and similarly, if they did drop back towards $230, I would view that as an attractive entry point.

data.title: Ulta Beauty: Earnings Inflection Catalyzed, Initiate At Buy
data.description:
Key thesis

We initiate coverage on (NASDAQ:ULTA) with a Buy rating and $432.81 price target. Ulta Beauty is the largest dedicated beauty retail network in the U.S. providing cosmetics, skincare, salon services, and private-label products in over 1,300 stores and strong digital channels. Our Buy thesis is driven by:

1) We model a purposeful -10.4% EPS contraction in FY25 as ULTA absorbs significant investments in its "Ulta Beauty Unleashed" strategy, planting the seed for an explosive FY26 EPS recovery that we model 7.3% ahead of consensus.

2) We believe the Street is materially underestimating the EPS leverage from ULTA's high-margin e-commerce marketplace, 44.6M-member loyalty program, and $200M-$250M in cost optimizations, which we build into our above-consensus FY26 EPS forecasts.

3) We use a FY25E blended PE of 16.5X to value the shares, with the FY25E representing NT headwinds, blending to 20.0X FY26E as investor confidence improves and the margin inflection arrives. Near-term margin pressures and modest share loss are downside risks but are temporary in nature and included in our blended PE calculation. All in, we see a highly attractive risk/reward profile, with current weakness offering a strong entry point ahead of the EPS acceleration.

What if Ulta's near-term pain is, in fact, the exact catalyst for the long-term resumption of its beauty retail dominance?

We forecast a purposeful step back in earnings, assigning FY25 EPS of $22.70 —midpoint of mgmt. guidance & a -10.4% contraction over FY24 —as Ulta absorbs the seismic investment of "Ulta Beauty Unleashed". By comparison, consensus is calling for $22.84 in FY26; we see recovery arriving sooner & stronger — our FY26E EPS forecast of $24.50 puts us +7.3% above the Street, driven by our belief that this year's operational headwinds will become competitive tailwinds (Street Consensus Forecast). Why the upside?

1) We believe the full-year impact of Ulta's new e-comm marketplace will drive incremental high-margin top-line growth via loyalty integration & a wider, lower-risk assortment.

2) The cost-optimization actions targeting $200-250M over three years will reach full impact in FY26, further compressing SG&A and driving operating margin expansion at an inflection point.

3) The razor-thin market share loss of 2024 is the foundation for a bolder comeback as exclusive launches, digital acceleration, and wellness expansion rekindle urgency among a resilient base of 44.6M loyalty members. In short, we believe FY25’s necessary earnings trough is the launchpad: those willing to endure a contraction now will, in our view, see earnings surge ahead of peers and consensus as Ulta’s strategic revamp pays off in FY26.

Source : FMP FY26 FY27
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $11.58B 21 $12.13B 26
EBITDA $1.83B 21 $1.91B 26
Net Income $1.12B 21 $1.21B 26
EPS $22.84 21 $25.36 23
SG&A Expenses $2.83B 21 $2.96B 26

Loyalty Program Monetization: Ulta’s Data Moat & The Engine for LTV

The scale and sophistication of the Ulta Beauty loyalty program is not merely a reflection of brand affinity—it is a powerful, underestimated catalyst for monetization and future margin expansion. With 44.6M members, Ulta boasts the most exhaustive data asset in beauty, providing a granular view of guest preferences, behaviors, and brand interactions at a scale rarely matched by peers. This data foundation enables a level of real-time, automated personalization (via Adobe’s CDP) that provides a foundation for true individualization, versus the more superficial, less scalable forms of activation more common at peers.

Importantly, the strategy is not limited to the activation of incremental purchases, but rather explicitly leverages analytics and ML to optimize for lifetime value and curate journeys that drive engagement, experimentation across categories. This strategy is essential because it can translate directly into higher frequency, larger baskets, and greater cross-channel engagement, and it can become ever more important as competitive pressures build and as digital and marketplace offerings become a larger piece of the model. In other words, the loyalty program is not a traditional points engine, but rather a strategic asset that will increasingly help enable above-peer margin recovery by deepening guest engagement, loyalty, and the effectiveness of new digital initiatives.

E-Commerce Marketplace Leverage: Assortment, Agility, and Margin Expansion

The imminent launch of Ulta Beauty's Mirakl-powered e-commerce marketplace is a game changer in our view. It fundamentally alters the economics of scaling the assortment, and the ability to capture incremental revenue without the margin dilution and working capital needs of inventory expansion. By launching a curated digital platform open to both emerging and established third-party beauty/wellness/lifestyle brands, Ulta is set to expand the depth and breadth of its product offering to guests, while also speeding the company's ability to respond to emerging trends and shifts in consumer demand across the market. This “endless aisle” capability should markedly differentiate Ulta from its physical-first competitors and also removes the inventory and fulfillment risk from the company, as third-party sellers will carry the inventory risk.

Valuation

Our $432.81 PT (+12% vs. NM) is based on blended Fwd P/E methodology of 16.5x FY25E & 20.0x FY26E, based on our above-consensus blended EPS of $23.63. We anchor our multiple path in confidence that FY25 will be a purposeful earnings trough (-10.4% guided contraction) as mgmt. doubles-down on the “Ulta Beauty Unleashed” investment cycle, with FY26 positioned as in inflectional EPS recovery off trough as cost levers and omni investments bear fruit. Our multiple selections (vs. peer & own multi-year medians) are below its own history (22.6x) and in line with peer medians (20.7x), though we argue this is overly conservative on transitory headwinds and discounts the resiliency of its 44.6M member base.

Importantly, the Street appears to be overly focused on near-term noise and not considering the structural earnings leverage from the maturing digital marketplace and cost engine (manifesting most noticeably in FY26). Our multiple selection intentionally strikes a balance in this debate, as we believe the discount to peer (-28%) and own multi-year avg. is unsustainable as earnings momentum returns, and patient investors underwrite FY25’s reset to capture multi-year re-rating as Ulta pivots from invest to accelerating earnings.

Risks to Our Buy Call

Our Buy thesis on Ulta Beauty is based on a transitory near-term earnings trough driven by strategic investment, and a forceful FY26 earnings rebound, but there are material risks around both the timing, and magnitude of the inflection.

First, the competitive landscape remains highly dynamic and intense, with U.S. market share pressure from Sephora's expansion in Kohl's, new beauty entrants at mass and off-price, and Amazon's vertical intensification leading to 1st-ever reported share losses in both prestige & mass makeup since '24, with >80% of stores impacted by competitor openings. If "Unleashed" investments fail to re-invigorate category growth, or promotional intensity persists, the Street may further de-rate MT operating margin and EPS expectations, particularly if traffic & ticket momentum continue to weaken beyond FY25.

Second, macro pressures that have been building for some time, including softening discretionary spend in the context of high consumer debt burdens and cautious consumer sentiment, have started to pressure performance, with earlier and deeper sales deceleration than expected in higher-margin prestige beauty and hair care, which could put our above-consensus recovery forecast at risk to downside surprise if trade-down, or reversion to the mean in demand patterns intensifies.

While we remain confident in the long-term competitive moats & monetization of the loyalty asset, we acknowledge that the timing and magnitude of EPS inflection is dependent on successfully navigating these headwinds, thus share trends, GM recovery, loyalty engagement, and comp trends will be key N-MT monitors for risk to our upside case.

Conclusion

We reiterate our Buy on Ulta Beauty, viewing the earnings trough as a conscious cyclical reset rather than structural deterioration, setting the stage for significant outperformance in FY26+. In our view, the market is underestimating the latent earnings power of Ulta's 44.6M-member loyalty platform and high-margin upside embedded in the digital marketplace and cost optimization initiatives—catalysts that will drive a faster and stronger recovery than consensus expects.

data.title: Federal Realty: A Solid Q1 Provides Scope For Faster Dividend Increases (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Federal Realty (NYSE:FRT) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing nearly 8% of their value as its premium valuation has given shares less ability to withstand the higher for longer interest rate environment. Valuation kept me at a “hold” when I last covered FRT in November 2023, and since then, the stock has returned 10%, well below the market’s 24% return. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if the valuation is sufficiently compelling to make FRT a buy. I am more bullish.

In the company’s first quarter, Federal Realty generated $1.70 in funds from operations (FFO), beating consensus by a penny while growing revenue by 6% from last year to $309 million. It is important to note that despite its name, Federal Realty does not have exposure to the federal government, which is a critical distinction given DOGE’s effort to cut the government’s real estate footprint. Rather, FRT owns open-air shopping centers.

This asset class has performed fairly well within real estate, far better than closed-air centers like malls. These spaces are typically anchored by grocers, and TJ Maxx (TJX) is its biggest tenant. This provides steady foot traffic and lessened exposure to e-commerce shifts. Additionally, there has been extremely little new construction over the past five years, tightening the market and supporting solid rental gains.

Importantly, FRT has a high-quality asset base. It owns over 100 properties, and its operations are focused in New York, DC, Boston, Southern California, Philadelphia, Chicago, and Miami. Relative to other public companies, FRT’s footprint is exposed to the highest-income and densest populations.

This is a significant positive; denser populations provide more foot traffic opportunities. Plus, with its higher-income consumers, demand should be more resilient. Especially with the potential for tariffs to increase inflation, lower income consumers may face more pressure, given limited savings. I would expect spending from higher-income households to hold up better during any economic slowdown.

While FRT is primarily a retail REIT, it does have mixed-use properties, which are home to apartments and offices alongside the core shopping center. This also helps provide more foot traffic to stores in its key retail operation. Overall, though, over three-quarters of the business is tied to retail leasing.

In Q1, we saw the benefits of favorable supply/demand dynamics, though expenses do continue to pressure margin. In its same-property portfolio, there was 93.6% occupancy, up 180bps from a year ago but down 10bps sequentially. 95.9% of properties were leased, up 160bps from last year. When a new tenant signs a lease, they typically do not start paying rent right away, as they are given some time to update the footprint and get the space store-ready. That is why there is a 230bp gap between leasing and occupancy. This is a fairly wide gap, and as we see leased spaces become occupied, that will provide a natural tailwind for revenue.

Looking within key components, its anchor leased rate rose by 100bps to 96.8% but was down 70bps sequentially. This is still an extremely strong level, consistent with the limited supply of anchor space in desirable locations. Its small shop leased rate was 93.5%, up 210bps from last year. I view any level above 92% as extremely strong. Small shops tend to turn over more quickly than anchors. These smaller tenants pay more, as anchors are able to negotiate a discount given their size and traffic benefits. Continuing to push up leasing at small shops is particularly accretive to cash flow. I am cautious on the ability for further improvement beyond current levels, given how strong they are.

There also continues to be solid demand for space. This is evidenced not just by Federal Realty’s occupancy, but by the fact that new leases it signed were at $40.63 per square foot, representing a 6% increase in cash rates. With market leases running above the portfolio’s current lease rate, renewals provide an ongoing source of cash flow growth.

Thanks to these factors, same-property operating income (NOI) was up 2.8% to $189 million. Revenue rose by 4%, at the high-end of the 3-4% rental revenue growth, I believe, is attainable over time. However, same-property expenses rose by 7%. As such, income grew 120bps more slowly than revenue. Operating expenses have run hot, industrywide, driven by a tight supply of labor and increasing insurance costs. I expect insurance costs to remain elevated, but we are likely past the peak, given trends in premiums as you can see below, while a slight softening in the labor market should help slow wage costs.

Outside of its core retail operations, I would note that the residential leased rate at its ~3,100 apartments is 94.9%, a healthy level given the national vacancy rate is 7%. With interest rates remaining elevated and home affordability challenged, I see a favorable outlook for multifamily demand. Indeed, residential rents rose just under 2% to $27 million in the quarter. Plus, the number of units under construction has fallen considerably from its peak. With new supply coming down, rental inflation should accelerate. Separately, over 96% of its office portfolio is leased, at an average 8-year term, so I also see limited risk here.

Beyond core results, the company completed a $123 million acquisition of the Del Monte Shopping Center in California. During the quarter, it also invested $37 million of cap-ex on its same-property portfolio, over 85% of which was for redevelopment. Importantly, the company maintains a strong balance sheet with $4.5 billion of debt, for just 52.4% debt to capital. As you can see, it also has no significant 2025 maturities, though its 2026 refinancing will almost certainly be at a higher rate, providing a ~ $13 million annual headwind to cash flow, or about $0.15 a share. Rolling over low-cost debt is likely to be an earnings headwind for much of the REIT sector over the medium term.

Given its strong balance sheet and underperforming share price, Federal Realty authorized a $300 million buyback program. It also pays a 4.6% dividend yield. There have been 57 straight years of dividend growth, and I expect this streak to continue, given its strong asset positioning, solid balance sheet, and favorable coverage.

Alongside results, FRT slightly bumped up guidance. FRT now expects $7.11-$7.23 in FFO, representing 6% growth from $7.10-$7.22. Given my view that expense pressure should slow, I see $7.15-$7.25 in FFO this year. At the midpoint, this provides a very strong 1.6x coverage ratio. This coverage positions FRT to retain ~$240 million per year of cash flow, which can fully fund its capital spending needs and provide capacity for modest bolt-on M&A.

In this sector, I view 1.4x coverage as a strong level. That would mean FRT has a dividend capacity of about $5.14 a share, about 17% above current levels. Because of this, I believe FRT has the capacity to raise its dividend slightly faster than FFO over time. Over the past five years, FRT has been growing its dividend just about 1% per year, which is why we have seen it move into this position of having extremely strong coverage. With a solid Q1 and 2025 shaping up to be a strong year, I believe FRT can be positioned to accelerate dividend growth when it does its annual increase later this year.

Given their prolonged underperformance, FRT’s valuation is no longer excessive, at less than 14x FFO and with a ~5.4% “potential” dividend yield using my normalized 1.4x coverage ratio. Shares now have a 7.6% FFO yield, while a peer like Brixmor (BRX) is at ~8.5%. I do believe a premium multiple is merited given FRT’s larger size, higher-quality locations, and exposure to apartments. Whereas I view 7.5% as a fair value yield for BRX, I view FRT’s fair value at 7%, given a 3-4% long-term growth profile. That suggests fair value is about $103.

This provides shares with a 12% total return opportunity, which I view as attractive. As such, I think FRT’s valuation has de-risked enough to make it a buy, especially with some support from share repurchases. Accordingly, after these Q1 results, I am upgrading FRT to a “buy” and view it as an attractive opportunity for income and moderate capital appreciation potential.
